text:
# Differential Machine Learning for 0DTE Options with Stochastic Volatility and Jumps

Takayuki Sakuma<sup>∗</sup> Faculty of Economics and Business Administration, Soka University

July 14, 2026

##### **Abstract**

We present a differential machine learning method for zero-days-to-expiry (0DTE) options under a stochastic-volatility jump-diffusion model. To handle the ultra-short-maturity regime, we express the option price in Black–Scholes form with a maturity-gated variance correction, combining supervision on prices and Greeks with a PIDE-residual penalty. Prices and Greeks are derived from a single trained pricing network, while jump-term identifiability is ensured by a jump-operator network fitted jointly in a three-stage procedure. The method improves jump-term approximation relative to one-stage baselines while maintaining comparable pricing errors. Furthermore, it reduces errors in Greeks, produces stable one-day delta hedges, and offers significant speedups over Fourier-based benchmarks. Calibration experiments demonstrate the network’s efficiency as a pricer and incorporating jump-intensity price sensitivity into the learning process further improves the overall model fit. We also consider a jump rough Heston model.

## **1 Introduction**

Zero-days-to-expiry (0DTE) options have grown rapidly over the past few years and now account for a large share of trading volume. Recent empirical work documents frequent intraday jumps in 0DTE markets [Bozovic(2025)], studies the role of gamma-related open-interest exposures [Dim et al.(2024)], and analyzes the pricing implications of short-horizon tail risk in 0DTE options [Bandi et al.(2023)]. These findings raise two practical challenges: first, the underlying dynamics may be better described by a diffusion model with jumps; second, the very short maturities and frequent intraday rebalancing demand fast computation of option prices and Greeks.

We apply differential machine learning (DML) [Huge and Savine(2020), Frandsen et al.(2022)] to 0DTE options under the Bates stochastic-volatility jump-diffusion (SVJD) model. Learning-based models can reduce pricing costs: once trained, prices and Greeks are obtained from a single network evaluation. However, the numerically most challenging region is near the money, where Greeks can become very large for 0DTE options with jumps. A common approach in machine-learning PDE solvers is to enforce the governing PDE by penalizing its residual at sampled state points, together with terminal and boundary conditions [Raissi et al.(2019)]. Several recent studies apply this residual-penalty approach to option pricing with jumps by including a PIDE-residual term in the training objective [Fu and Hirsa(2020), Sun et al.(2025), Bansal et al.(2026)]. Three design choices are central to our approach:

1. We adopt DML, training a single price network jointly on option values and Greeks. The Greeks are obtained by automatic differentiation of the network output with respect to its inputs and enter the loss directly.

2. Instead of predicting prices directly, the network learns a variance correction inside a Black–Scholes formula [Black and Scholes(1973)], scaled so that the correction vanishes as _τ →_ 0. This preserves the correct short-maturity limit (the payoff) and reduces the approximation burden in the near-singular

> ∗e-mail: tsakuma@soka.ac.jp.

region. More broadly, this design is related to neural-network surrogates for option pricing and impliedvolatility computation [Liu et al.(2019)], and to implied-volatility smoothing methods that impose no-arbitrage structure to improve surface regularity [Ackerer et al.(2020)].

3. We introduce a second neural network to represent the compensated jump operator. If the jump component is identified only through a PIDE-residual penalty, the optimizer can trade off diffusion and jump errors while keeping the overall residual small. A small residual therefore does not guarantee that the learned jump operator matches the model-implied jump integral. The second network makes the residual penalty informative about the jump contribution.

Recent calibration studies using DML fall into two broad strands. Sridi and Bilokon (2023) apply DML to the calibration of vanilla European puts under the Heston model, while Polala and Hientzsch (2023) extend parametric DML to joint pricing and calibration problems [Sridi and Bilokon(2023), Polala and Hientzsch(2023)]. In these approaches, DML primarily replaces the pricer to gain speed. Zhang et al. (2025), by contrast, propose a gradient-based scheme that learns both prices and sensitivities with respect to model parameters [Zhang et al.(2025)]. We ask whether our approach can play both roles: as a fast pricer in calibration and as a way to improve calibration in a focused extension. We also ask whether our DML framework can be extended to rough volatility models. To this end, we consider a jump rough Heston model based on a multi-factor Markovian approximation, which replaces the fractional kernel by a finite sum of exponentials [Abi Jaber and El Euch(2019)].

**Paper organization.** Section 2 presents the Bates stochastic-volatility jump-diffusion model. Section 3 introduces the DML-based neural network and the three-stage training scheme. Section 4 specifies the loss functions and constraints. Section 5 reports numerical experiments, including calibration exercises.

## **2 Bates model**

We work with the Bates stochastic-volatility jump-diffusion (SVJD) model, which combines a Heston-type variance process with Merton-style lognormal price jumps [Merton(1976), Heston(1993), Bates(1996)]. For brevity, we refer to this simply as the Bates model throughout the paper. Under the risk-neutral measure, let _St_ denote the underlying asset, _Vt_ the instantaneous variance, _r_ the risk-free rate, and _q_ the dividend. The dynamics are

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0002-06.png)

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0002-07.png)

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0002-09.png)

with terminal condition _C_ ( _S, V,_ 0) = ( _S − K_ )<sup>+</sup> . The compensated jump operator is

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0002-11.png)

where _fY_ denotes the density of the logarithmic jump size _Y_ . We use the dimensionless log-moneyness

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0002-13.png)

and the diffusion part of the operator is

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-01.png)

where _ut_ = _−uτ_ since _τ_ is time-to-maturity. We define the residual

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-03.png)

where _Jψ_ denotes a neural approximation to the normalized compensated jump operator

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-05.png)

In the architecture below, the second network outputs _Jψ_ directly and is supervised against a numerical quadrature proxy for _Jψ_ ( **x** ).

## **3 DML for PIDEs**

Figure 1 summarizes the model architecture. The solid arrows correspond to forward evaluations while dashed arrows indicate computations derived from these outputs. The input is

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-09.png)

Rather than predicting prices directly, the first network outputs a variance correction ∆ _Vϕ_ ( **x** ). We define an effective variance

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-11.png)

and we return a Black–Scholes call price with volatility _σ_ eff =<sup>_√_</sup> _V_ eff . The function _g_ ( _τ_ ) forces the learned variance correction to vanish near expiry, which stabilizes training and preserves the payoff limit. Since we work in log-moneyness, we normalize the strike to _K_ = 1:

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-13.png)

and Greeks are obtained by automatic differentiation of _uϕ_ . In parallel, a jump-operator network approximates the compensated jump operator _Jψ_ ( **x** ), and the two networks are coupled through the jump-PIDE residual. Because the jump term is not identifiable from a residual loss alone, we supervise _Jψ_ and use the three-stage schedule described in Subsection 3.2.

### **3.1 Twin network**

A standard supervised model fits a network _uϕ_ ( **x** ) by

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-17.png)

DML augments this objective by adding supervised targets for selected Greeks _∂u/∂xi_ :

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-19.png)

### **3.1 Twin network** A standard supervised model fits a network _uϕ_ ( **x** ) by ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-17.png) DML augments this objective by adding supervised targets for selected Greeks _∂u/∂xi_ : ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0003-19.png) where _∂xiuϕ_ are computed by automatic differentiation and _I_ indexes the Greeks of interest (e.g., delta, gamma, and vega). Following Huge and Savine (2020), we use the term “twin network” to denote a single price network paired with Greeks obtained by automatic differentiation. Figure 1: Architecture and training procedure. Stages 1–3 describe the three-stage training scheme. A variance-correction network returns ∆ _Vϕ_ ( **x** ), multiplied by a deterministic maturity function _g_ ( _τ_ ) so that the correction vanishes as _τ →_ 0. Prices are produced by substituting the resulting effective variance into the Black–Scholes call formula, yielding _uϕ_ ( **x** ) (not the standard BS price unless ∆ _Vϕ ≡_ 0). A separate network outputs the compensated jump contribution _Jψ_ ( **x** ). Greeks are obtained by automatic differentiation of _uϕ_ . The jump-PIDE residual _R_ ( **x** ) is computed and penalized at randomly sampled points. Stages 1–3 depict the three-stage training scheme used for jump-term identifiability. Training can be further regularized by adding a PDE-residual penalty computed from the same automaticdifferentiation derivatives. However, if we approximate the nonlocal jump operator _J_ [ _u_ ] by a separate network _Jψ_ ( **x** ) and use it only through the residual ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0004-03.png) _uϕ_ and _Jψ_ are not separately identifiable: in principle, for any _uϕ_ one can set _Jψ_ = _−L_ diff _uϕ/λ_ and obtain _R ≡_ 0. This mechanism can lead to a degenerate solution in which the jump network cancels diffusionoperator errors and reduces the residual without learning a meaningful jump contribution. We therefore supervise the jump operator explicitly. Using reference prices _u_ref , we construct a numerical proxy for the jump term and train _Jψ_ to match it. ### **3.2 Three-stage training with jump supervision** As noted in Section 3.1, _Jψ_ can act as a residual-cancelling degree of freedom, absorbing diffusion-operator errors without approximating the jump operator. We therefore use a three-stage schedule: 1. **Stage 1 (price and Greeks).** Train the price network while freezing _Jψ_ , using the price and Greek terms plus the no-arbitrage penalties. 2. **Stage 2 (jump reference).** Freeze the price network and train the jump network to match a numerical proxy of the compensated jump term computed from the reference prices: ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0004-09.png) On randomly sampled state points, we approximate the expectation in (12) with a Gauss–Hermite rule applied to _u_ref ( _x_ + _Y, V, τ_ ) and penalize _Jψ − J_ref ( _x, V, τ_ ) using a Huber loss function. 3. **Stage 3 (joint refinement).** Train both networks jointly. In this stage we retain weak supervision against _J_ref ( _x, V, τ_ ) and add a _self-consistency_ regularizer that penalizes the mismatch between _Jψ_ and a low-order numerical jump evaluation based on the current price network. ## **4 Neural network and training** We use two fully-connected feedforward networks, each with width 192, depth 4, and SiLU activation functions (152,834 parameters in total). ### **4.1 Loss function, constraints, and weights** � Let _u_ = _uϕ_ ( **x** ) and (∆� _,_ Γ� _,_ � _ν_ ) denote the corresponding Greeks. We minimize the weighted objective ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0005-06.png) with: - _w_ ( **x** ) upweights the ATM region and short maturities, ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0005-09.png) - _m_ ( **x** ) = I( _u_ref ( **x** ) _>_ 10_−_4 ) drops Greek-loss contributions in deep OTM regions.

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0005-06.png)

with:

- _w_ ( **x** ) upweights the ATM region and short maturities,

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0005-09.png)

- _m_ ( **x** ) = I( _u_<sup>ref</sup> ( **x** ) _>_ 10<sup>_−_4</sup> ) drops Greek-loss contributions in deep OTM regions.

- _P_ NA encodes simple static no-arbitrage constraints: delta bounds (0 _≤_ ∆ _≤_ 1), convexity ( _uxx − ux ≥_ 0), and vega monotonicity ( _ν ≥_ 0), implemented as squared positive-part penalties ( _·_ )<sup>2</sup> +<sup>,where</sup> ( _z_ )+ = max _{z,_ 0 _}_ .

- Greek scaling uses _λg ≈_ 1 _/_ ~~�~~ E[ _g_<sup>2</sup> ] estimated once from the training set.

**Self-consistency penalty.** During joint training we augment (13) with a penalty that encourages the learned jump network to agree with a low-order numerical evaluation of the compensated operator applied to the current price network:

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0005-14.png)

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0005-15.png)

where ( _zi, wi_ )<sup>_n_</sup> _i_ =1<sup>SCarenodesandweightsofaGauss–Hermiterulefor</sup><sup>_Z∼N_(0</sup><sup>_,_1),andweuse</sup><sup>_ω_SC=</sup> 0 _._ 05 _, n_ SC = 16.

**Robust** Γ **(gamma) loss and numerical filtering.** For the most sensitive derivative targets we may replace the squared error in (13) with a robust Huber loss and ignore samples where the reference violates convexity due to numerical quadrature error. We adopt a Huber loss to reduce the impact of isolated quadrature-induced outliers, which is especially relevant for Γ and the jump-term. For Γ this takes the form

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0005-18.png)

The training stages correspond to different restrictions of the full objective:

- **Stage 1:** minimize (13) using only the price and Greek terms and _P_ NA (i.e. set _ωR_ = _ω_ col = 0 and freeze _ψ_ ).

- **Stage 2:** freeze _ϕ_ and minimize a Huber loss on the jump network, E[Huber( _Jψ − J_<sup>ref</sup> ( _x, V, τ_ ))], where _J_<sup>ref</sup> is computed from the reference Fourier pricer (Section 3.2).

- **Stage 3:** jointly refine both networks with the full loss (13) (small _ωR_ ) plus weak jump supervision and the self-consistency penalty (14). For the Γ target we use the robust variant (16).

## **5 Numerical experiments**

Our experiments address four questions: how DML and residual regularization affect 0DTE price/Greek accuracy; whether the learned jump term is actually identified; whether the resulting prices and Greeks remain reliable in hedging tests; and whether a parameter-gradient extension can improve calibration. Additional model-choice robustness checks (BS/Merton baselines and an SVCJ extension) are reported in Appendix A.

### **5.1 Setting**

Benchmark prices and Greeks are generated by a Fourier-transform pricer for the Bates model (1024-point quadrature; cutoff _u_ max = 1000) [Carr and Madan(1999)]. Reference Greeks are computed by automatic differentiation of the same implementation.

- Log-moneyness: with probability _p_ core = 0 _._ 7 we draw from a near-ATM band _x ∼_ Unif[ _−_ 0 _._ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 1] (“ATM core”); otherwise _x ∼_ Unif[ _−_ 0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _._ 5].

- Maturity (strict 0DTE): we oversample the shortest maturities. With probability _p_ short = 0 _._ 7 we draw _τ ∼_ Unif[10<sup>_−_4</sup> _,_ 1 _/_ 504]; otherwise _τ ∼_ Unif[1 _/_ 504 _,_ 1 _/_ 252]. Here _p_ short is the mixture weight for that oversampling step.

- Parameters are sampled uniformly over the following ranges:

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0006-10.png)

To stabilize jump-term training, we augment the sampled states by pushing points through the jump map _x �→ x_ + _Y_ and clamping to _|x| ≤_ 6. We compute reference prices on these jump-shifted (“margin”) points and add a small auxiliary price-consistency loss. This extends supervision beyond _|x| ≤_ 0 _._ 5 to the region visited by the jump integral. Deep OTM options have tiny prices and noisy Greeks, so we ignore Greek targets when the reference price is below 10<sup>_−_4</sup> .

We use _ωG_ = 0 _._ 7, _ωR_ = 0 _._ 01, _ω_ col = 0 _._ 1, _ω_ NA = 0 _._ 05, and ( _ω_ ∆ _, ω_ Γ _, ων_ ) = (1 _._ 0 _,_ 0 _._ 3 _,_ 0 _._ 5). We also set the self-consistency weight _ω_ SC = 0 _._ 05 with _n_ SC = 16. Finally, we set ( _W_ ATM _, W_ SHORT _, W_ ATM&SHORT) = (1 _._ 0 _,_ 1 _._ 0 _,_ 1 _._ 0).

In the ultra-short regime, second derivatives are numerically unstable. We therefore (i) ignore deep-OTM Greek targets, (ii) use robust losses and/or clipping for sensitive targets (Γ and the jump term), and (iii) add no-arbitrage shape penalties, particularly convexity ( _uxx − ux ≥_ 0).

### **5.2 Accuracy across models**

We compare four models:

1. **A (price-only)** : train on prices only (no Greek loss, no residual penalty).

### **5.2 Accuracy across models**

We compare four models:

1. **A (price-only)** : train on prices only (no Greek loss, no residual penalty).

2. **B (DML)** : train on prices and Greeks (via automatic differentiation), with no residual penalty.

3. **C (DML+residual)** : train on prices, Greeks, and the residual penalty (13).

#### 4. **D (three-stage)** : three-stage training with jump supervision (Section 3.2).

We train the one-stage variants (Models A–C) for 100 epochs with Adam (learning rate 10<sup>_−_3</sup> ) and batch size 64. The three-stage model uses 100 epochs (Stage 1), 60 epochs (Stage 2), and 30 epochs (Stage 3). Table 1 reports global RMSE, and Table 2 reports RMSE in the region _|x| <_ 0 _._ 05 and _τ ≤_ 1 _/_ 252. Residual statistics are in Table 3.

We additionally report scale-invariant metrics and tail error quantiles in Table 4. For price RMSE, we use two scale-free metrics:

For Greeks we use

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0007-04.png)

The tail metrics _|eP |p_ and _|e_ Γ _|p_ denote the _p_ -th percentile of the absolute price and gamma errors, respectively. Tables 1–4 suggest three points. First, Model B improves delta accuracy (first-order risk) and vega accuracy relative to the price-only fit (Model A) (Table 1). For gamma Γ, improvements are clearer in tail error quantiles than in global RMSE (Table 4). Second, the one-stage PIDE residual penalty (Model C) does not improve prices or Greeks relative to Model B. This is consistent with jump models, where the residual can be reduced via cancellation between the differential and jump terms rather than by learning an interpretable jump operator. Third, the three-stage model (Model D) yields the best overall ∆and vega RMSE among the Greek-supervised models and slightly improves Γ tail error quantiles, although Γ remains the most delicate target in the region ( _|x| <_ 0 _._ 05 _, τ ≤_ 1 _/_ 252).

Figure 2 visualizes true vs predicted prices and Greeks for Model D. In the ultra-short maturity regime ( _τ →_ 0), Γ is extremely localized around the money: for the majority of (deep ITM/OTM) evaluation points the true Γ is numerically close to zero, so the scatter plots naturally show a dense cluster at Γ _≈_ 0.

### **5.3 Jump-term comparison**

This subsection clarifies why a small PIDE residual alone is not evidence that the learned jump component represents the intended compensated jump integral. We first report error distributions for the final threestage model (Figure 3), and then directly test whether the learned compensated jump contribution _Jψ_ matches a numerical proxy computed from the reference pricer (Figure 4).

We focus on the domain _|x| ≤_ 0 _._ 5. Despite its small PIDE residual, the residual-regularized model C (DML+residual) without jump supervision shows a large mismatch between _Jψ_ and _J_<sup>ref</sup> (RMSE = 9 _._ 34 _×_ 10<sup>_−_2</sup> ). The three-stage model yields a smaller jump-term error (RMSE = 1 _._ 35 _×_ 10<sup>_−_2</sup> ). Price RMSE on the same points is comparable (C: 7 _._ 17 _×_ 10<sup>_−_3</sup> , D: 5 _._ 89 _×_ 10<sup>_−_3</sup> ). Therefore, residual magnitude alone is not sufficient for model selection when diffusion and jump terms can offset each other.

This indicates that when the jump contribution is a free network output, it can absorb approximation errors from the diffusion part and still drive the residual toward zero. Supervising _Jψ_ against a numerical proxy (three-stage schedule) improves identification of the jump contribution.

### **5.4 One-day delta-hedging**

The data-generating process is the Bates model, and we simulate _n_ paths = 5000 paths of ( _St, Vt_ ) over one trading day _T_ = 1 _/_ 252 using an Euler scheme with _n_ steps = 24. Jumps are simulated by a Bernoulli approximation with step probability _p ≈ λ_ ∆ _t_ . To avoid unrealistically large per-step jump probabilities when _λ_ ∆ _t_ is not very small, we cap this probability at 0 _._ 2, i.e. we use _p_ = min( _λ_ ∆ _t,_ 0 _._ 2). We use a stressed parameter set

![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0007-13.png)

chosen to amplify jump activity and gamma effects. First, we conduct stock-only ∆hedges, where the hedge portfolio contains the underlying and a cash account. We run the hedge separately for each strike _K ∈{_ 0 _._ 9 _,_ 1 _._ 0 _,_ 1 _._ 1 _}_ on the same set of simulated ( _St, Vt_ ) paths, yielding 5000 P&L outcomes per strike. At _t_ = 0, we short one call with strike _K ∈{_ 0 _._ 9 _,_ 1 _._ 0 _,_ 1 _._ 1 _}_ and maturity _T_ . At each rebalance time _tk_ , we

At maturity we record hedging P&L as the final portfolio value minus the option payoff. Aggregating across strikes (15000 paths total), we report summary statistics in Table 5. Figure 5 plots P&L histograms, showing that the distributional difference between true and model delta hedges is small. Table 5: One-day ∆-hedging P&L summary. |Model|Mean|Std|5%|Median|95%| |---|---|---|---|---|---| |True ∆|_−_1_._40_×_10_−_4|9_._92_×_10_−_3|_−_5_._60_×_10_−_4|+3_._20_×_10_−_4|+1_._63_×_10_−_3| |DML ∆|_−_1_._30_×_10_−_4|9_._92_×_10_−_3|_−_5_._00_×_10_−_4|+3_._50_×_10_−_4|+1_._59_×_10_−_3| Conditioning on whether at least one price jump occurs along the underlying path, we find much larger dispersion for the DML hedge on jump samples. ### **5.5 Calibration experiments** In the spirit of [Sridi and Bilokon(2023)] and [Polala and Hientzsch(2023)], we use the trained DML model as a fast pricing surrogate in calibration, but, for simplicity, calibrate only _β_ = ( _v_ 0 _, θ, λ_ ) while keeping the remaining parameters fixed. All calibration targets in this subsection are European calls with _S_ 0 = 1, _r_ = 0 _._ 01, _q_ = 0, and strikes parameterized by log-moneyness _x_ = log( _S_ 0 _/K_ ). For a panel _{_ ( _xi, τi, Pi_obs ) _}__N_ _i_ =1, we solve the weighted least-squares problem with L-BFGS-B ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0012-02.png) with _v_ 0 _, θ ∈_ [0 _._ 005 _,_ 0 _._ 5] and _λ ∈_ [0 _._ 01 _,_ 2 _._ 0]. Unless otherwise noted, we use ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0012-04.png) for the calibration weights. In Experiments 2–4, we also assign the short-maturity weight when _τi_ = 1 _/_ 252. Thus near-the-money calls receive weight 3, short-maturity calls receive weight 3, and calls satisfying both conditions receive weight 9. **Experiment 1.** The call panel contains 68 prices with parameters ( _v_ 0 _, θ, λ_ ) = (0 _._ 04 _,_ 0 _._ 04 _,_ 0 _._ 30). The calls lie on a log-moneyness grid _x ∈_ [ _−_ 0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _._ 5] with 17 equally spaced points and maturities _τ ∈{_ 0 _._ 25 _,_ 0 _._ 50 _,_ 0 _._ 75 _,_ 1 _._ 00 _}/_ 252. Replacing the direct Fourier pricer with the trained DML surrogate makes calibration about 90 _×_ faster. The surface fit remains accurate: the DML calibration yields price RMSE 1 _._ 53 _×_ 10_−_5 , versus 1 _._ 23 _×_ 10_−_7 for direct Fourier calibration. The fitted parameters, however, differ more noticeably. The DML calibration settles at _λ_ˆ = 0 _._ 20, while direct Fourier calibration recovers _λ_ˆ _≈_ 0 _._ 30. Even direct Fourier calibration does not recover the generating vector exactly, returning _θ_ˆ = 0 _._ 05. On such a short-maturity panel, exact parameter recovery is therefore difficult even when pricing error is tiny, a point reinforced by the identification results in Experiment 3. **Experiment 2.** We next examine how model misspecification manifests itself in calibration. The impliedvolatility surface is specified as ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0012-08.png) ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0012-09.png)

**Experiment 2.** We next examine how model misspecification manifests itself in calibration. The impliedvolatility surface is specified as ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0012-08.png) ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0012-09.png) with _a_ ( _τ_ ) = 0 _._ 18 + 0 _._ 03 _e__−_35_τ_ + 0 _._ 005 sin(150 _τ_ ), _b_ ( _τ_ ) = 0 _._ 20 + 0 _._ 10 _e__−_15_τ_ , and _c_ ( _τ_ ) = 0 _._ 08 + 0 _._ 05 _e__−_20_τ_ . We convert this surface to call prices, floor volatilities at 5% and cap them at 100% pointwise, and then fit the Bates model by Fourier-pricer calibration under the box constraints in (17). Figure 7 shows structured residuals across moneyness and maturity. The fit pushes _θ_ˆ to its lower bound 0 _._ 005, _λ_ˆ to its lower bound ˆ 0 _._ 01, and _v_ 0 to 0 _._ 0073. This pattern indicates that the optimizer suppresses both variance and jump activity in order to mimic a surface that lies outside the model class. ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0013-00.png) Experiment 2: pseudo-market residual heatmap

**Experiment 4.** Inspired by the parameter-sensitivity supervision in [Zhang et al.(2025)], we consider a parameter-gradient extension focused on jump intensity. We train two DML networks on calls generated by the Bates model: one on prices only, and one on prices together with the price sensitivity with respect to _λ_ . Letting _ξj_ denote the full network input for training sample _j_ , we train the sensitivity-augmented network with ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0014-01.png) where _ωλ_ = 20 and _gj_(_λ_) is the jump-intensity price-sensitivity label generated by automatic differentiation. Calibration still solves weighted least squares in price space: ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0014-03.png) Table 6 shows that Fourier pricer calibration recovers _λ_ˆ = 0 _._ 59997 for _λ_ true = 0 _._ 60. The price-only DML proxy overestimates jump intensity, yielding _λ_ˆ = 0 _._ 73878, whereas the price+ _λ_ -sensitivity DML moves the estimate to _λ_ˆ = 0 _._ 56889. We use the same mixed-expiry grid as Calibration Experiment 3 so that the jump-gradient calibration is not evaluated on a distinct expiry panel. The sensitivity-supervised surrogate does not exactly recover _λ_ , but it materially reduces the absolute error from about 0 _._ 139 to about 0 _._ 031. For ultra-short maturities, _λ_ is the parameter most directly tied to jump exposure, so supervising _∂λP_ appears helpful for calibrating that direction, even though it does not solve the full multi-parameter calibration problem by itself. Table 6: Gradient-based calibration in the jump-intensity direction. We replace the Markovian state ( _s, v, τ_ ) with an _M_ -factor Markovian approximation of rough volatility, so that the pricing network uses ( _s, y_ 1 _, . . . , yM , τ_ ) as input [Abi Jaber and El Euch(2019)]. Specifically, we approximate the fractional kernel ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0014-09.png) by a finite sum of exponentials, ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0014-11.png) where the coefficients _cm_ are estimated by nonnegative least squares. Let ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0014-13.png) and define ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0014-15.png) The _M_ -factor rough diffusion generator is ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0015-01.png) The jump-network target is the compensated price-jump operator per unit intensity,

where the coefficients _cm_ are estimated by nonnegative least squares. Let ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0014-13.png) and define ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0014-15.png) The _M_ -factor rough diffusion generator is ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0015-01.png) The jump-network target is the compensated price-jump operator per unit intensity, ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0015-03.png) where _κJ_ = E[ _e__Z_ _−_ 1]. The reference prices are computed using the rough-volatility variance-reduction method of McCrickerd and Pakkanen [McCrickerd and Pakkanen(2018)], combined with Merton’s lognormaljump mixture [Merton(1976)]. As in the Bates/SVJD-B experiment, Gauss–Hermite quadrature is used only to construct supervised labels for this compensated jump operator under the reference model. The pricing-equation residual uses the learned jump block _J_� _ϕ_ : ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0015-06.png) and the price network is trained on inputs (log( _S/K_ ) _, y_ 1 _, . . . , yM , τ_ ) with a loss function similar to (13): ![](assets/2026-sakuma-dml-0dte.pdf-0015-08.png) The term _L_ jump trains the learned jump block _J_� _ϕ_ against (23) and _P_ NA_M_denotestherough-stateanalogue of the no-arbitrage penalty _P_ NA in (13), applied to the _M_ -factor RHM-DML price surface _u_ � _θ_ ( _x, y, τ_ ). We generate a volatility surface from a higher-dimensional RHM with ( _M, H_ ) = (12 _,_ 0 _._ 07) and fit Bates/SVJD-B, SVCJ, and a lower-dimensional RHM approximation with ( _M, H_ ) = (4 _,_ 0 _._ 10). Table 7 reports implied-volatility RMSEs under three calibration grids. The RHM-with-jumps specification attains the lowest implied-volatility RMSE in all three grids, supporting the interpretation that rough memory helps reproduce the shape of the implied-volatility surface when combined with jumps. The jump operator is the compensated per-unit-intensity jump contribution learned by _J_� _ϕ_ . |Quantity|RMSE|Mean abs. error|q95 abs error|q99 abs error| |---|---|---|---|---| |Price|6_._80_×_10_−_4|5_._69_×_10_−_4|1_._31_×_10_−_3|1_._52_×_10_−_3| |Delta|7_._42_×_10_−_3|5_._32_×_10_−_3|1_._40_×_10_−_2|2_._53_×_10_−_2| |Gamma|4_._61_×_10_−_1|2_._15_×_10_−_1|1_._24|1_._85| |Jump operator|7_._78_×_10_−_4|6_._23_×_10_−_4|1_._67_×_10_−_3|2_._22_×_10_−_3|

Table 9 reports results for terminal one-step delta hedges. We choose the initial delta at _t_ = 0 and simulate 10 _,_ 000 terminal payoffs for a one-day ATM call with _τ_ = 1 _/_ 252. The Black–Scholes IV delta yields the smallest mean absolute P&L and RMSE, but the remaining tail losses indicate that a single stock-delta hedge cannot remove jump risk. Perfect hedging in rough Heston models is theoretically possible when the forward variance curve is available as a hedging instrument [El Euch and Rosenbaum(2018)]; more applied work formulates rough-volatility hedging through additional instruments or finite-factor partial hedging [Fukasawa et al.(2021), Motte and Hainaut(2024)]. Survey evidence also emphasizes that rough-volatility models may be valuable for pricing and risk management, but that practical implementation remains numerically and methodologically demanding [Hiraki and Shinozaki(2024)]. Rough models without jumps can struggle to reproduce wide-moneyness implied-volatility smiles, motivating a rough-plus-jump rather than a pure-rough comparison [Bandi et al.(2026)]. 2_._56_×_10_−_2| ## **6 Conclusion** We develop a differential machine learning framework for 0DTE options under the Bates model. The method embeds the price in a Black–Scholes representation with a maturity-gated variance correction and combines joint price-and-Greek supervision with jump-aware PIDE regularization. Across the numerical experiments, the three-stage design improves identification of the jump term while keeping price accuracy competitive, delta and vega errors low, and inference substantially faster than the Fourier benchmark. The calibration exercises show both the value and the limits of the approach: the network is a useful fast surrogate inside weighted least-squares calibration, but short-dated panels can still suffer from misspecification and weak parameter identification. Supervising jump-intensity sensitivity further improves recovery in this direction, reducing the jump-intensity calibration error relative to a price-only surrogate. The framework can also be extended to rough volatility models through a multi-factor Markovian approximation. Future research will extend the framework from simulation to market data. ## **Declaration of AI-assisted technologies** During the preparation of this manuscript, the author used generative AI and AI-assisted tools to assist with manuscript drafting, language refinement, code drafting and debugging, and discussion of the presentation of the research. ## **A Model comparison**

Because 0DTE options are extremely short-dated, it is natural to ask whether simpler dynamics would suffice for the pricing and hedging checks considered in this paper. This appendix reports two model-comparison checks: (i) Black–Scholes and Merton jump-diffusion baselines versus the Bates model, and (ii) an SVCJtype extension with contemporaneous jumps in price and variance, specified within the affine jump-diffusion framework of [Duffie et al.(2000)].

### **A.1 Black–Scholes and Merton versus the Bates model**

We use the Fourier pricer as a benchmark and compare two analytic baselines: (i) Black–Scholes with constant volatility _σ_ =<sup>_√_</sup> _<u>v</u>_ 0 and no jumps, and (ii) Merton’s lognormal jump-diffusion [Merton(1976)] with the _same_ jump parameters ( _λ, µJ , σJ_ ) and constant diffusion volatility<sup>_√_</sup> _<u>v</u>_ 0. The parameter set is the stressed regime used in the hedging experiment in the main text:

( _v_ 0 _, κ, θ, σv, ρ, λ, µJ , σJ_ ) = (0 _._ 04 _,_ 3 _._ 0 _,_ 0 _._ 04 _,_ 1 _._ 0 _, −_ 0 _._ 8 _,_ 2 _._ 0 _, −_ 0 _._ 05 _,_ 0 _._ 20) _,_

with _r_ = 1% and _q_ = 0.

Merton’s jump-diffusion substantially reduces pricing error relative to Black–Scholes (Table 10), indicating that even overnight options load on jump risk.

Table 10: Pricing error of Black–Scholes and Merton relative to Bates benchmark prices, evaluated on a 164point ( _x, τ_ ) grid. The second column reports RMSE on the core short-dated bucket ( _|x| <_ 0 _._ 05 _, τ ≤_ 1 _/_ 252) (92 grid points, including repeated endpoints).

|Model|Price RMSE (global)|Price RMSE (_|x| <_0_._05_, τ ≤_1_/_252)|
|---|---|---|
|Black–Scholes|0.000303|0.000297|
|Merton|0.000182|0.000121|

We also run a one-day discrete-time ∆hedge under simulated Bates dynamics (using the same setup as Section 5.4) and compute hedge deltas using BS, Merton, and the Bates model. Table 11 reports summary statistics, both unconditionally and conditional on whether at least one jump occurs along the path. Jump realizations dominate tail outcomes and cannot be hedged away by delta hedging; differences across delta models are therefore small relative to jump-driven tail risk in this setup.

### **A.2 SVCJ vs Bates: do volatility jumps matter for 0DTE?**

To assess whether volatility jumps matter for 0DTE options, we consider an SVCJ-type extension (stochastic volatility with contemporaneous jumps in price and variance) in which price and variance jump simultaneously at Poisson times. Within the affine jump-diffusion framework of [Duffie et al.(2000)], we specify the log-price jump as _Y ∼N_ ( _µJ , σJ_<sup>2)andthevariancejumpas</sup><sup>_Z∼_Exp(mean=</sup><sup>_µvJ_),with</sup><sup>_Z_independentof</sup> _Y_ but arriving with the same intensity _λ_ . We set _µvJ_ = 0 _._ 02 (a positive variance-jump mean), and keep ( _v_ 0 _, κ, θ, σv, ρ, λ, µJ , σJ_ ) equal to the stressed regime above. Table 12 shows that the incremental pricing effect of adding variance jumps is small. For very short maturities, vega exposure is limited; variance jumps can therefore be weakly identified unless longer expiries are included in calibration/training. The evaluation grid here uses four same-day maturity slices, _τ ∈{_ 0 _._ 25 _,_ 0 _._ 50 _,_ 0 _._ 75 _,_ 1 _._ 00 _} ×_ (1 _/_ 252). Accordingly, the metric “ATM” aggregates all grid points with _|x| <_ 0 _._ 1 across all four slices, whereas “ATM+short” additionally imposes _τ <_ 1 _/_ 252, i.e. it retains only the first three slices.

We conduct one-day delta hedging under simulated SVCJ dynamics and compare hedges based on Bates vs SVCJ deltas. Table 13 shows that the P&L distributions are very similar at the reported precision.

Table 11: One-day discrete-time ∆-hedging P&L under simulated Bates dynamics. “jump” means the path contains at least one price jump during the day; “no-jump” means no price jump occurs. The last column reports a nonparametric 95% bootstrap CI for CVaR1%.
